moment_text: Yeah, you know, I think I look at at it more like. I was building a layer cake. There wasn't just one single inflection point. I think this might be true for a lot of us, but three of those layers that have come to be true for me. The first was that when I was in my twenties, my father was diagnosed with young onset dementia. Um, and I watched my mom and I supported her as primary caregiver through the dementia process. So if anyone has ever had a family member go through the dementia process, you can, um, relate. It's long and grueling and importantly lonely. Um, and that will ring true later in, um, the third inflection point. So, um, so. That happened. I started exploring, educating myself and um, as you heard in my bio, then started exploring age tech. The second layer on that was, um, I became a foster parent in 2020 and had the experience of having someone else's child in my home and providing the best love and care that I could for that child in that moment, but really understanding what it meant for that child to be in the system. And how many points of trauma occurred, not only the initial trauma that pulled them from the home, but how many points of trauma occurred after that and why? The foster care system and the things that are attached to it, such as homelessness and incarceration are a cyclical process. Um, and then the third layer on that is I moved to a new city on my own in Northern California for a year. Um, and. As someone who was highly social, always connected, actually tried to push away, you know, more of an introvert, push away and be alone. I was really lonely. And so for the first time I had that glimpse into loneliness. And so all of this kind of came together in various levels of research and curiosity, um, into exploring what intergenerational communities. Are what they look like and what the benefits are. Um, and I think it's all these different experiences in my life that kind of led me here. And I see layers continue to happen every single day. Um, but everything tends to point back to building this intergenerational community.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains barrier signals (social_isolation) and emotional signals (loneliness) related to a family system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker describes being in her twenties when her father was diagnosed, which places this initial experience in the 'reproductive_years' life stage.

moment_text: Absolutely. Yeah. It's unique there. Um, but, um, man, the. Ambiguous loss and the daily new version of grief that happens with dementia. Any neurological? Um, I think maybe it happens with physical loss as well. I'm not as familiar with that, but, um, my dad became a different person and so I mourned the loss of my dad that I grew up knowing and then every day he would change. Um, and it d it can be pretty extreme that every, you know, every time I would see him, he was a different person. And so there's a lot of grief involved in that process. Um, and what's interesting about age related disease, nobody wants to talk about it. And I don't wanna say that any, any condition is a better condition to have than others. But, you know, a disease that feels like you can win it or you can come out the other side. People will talk to you about it. They'll get excited. They'll cheer for you. They'll raise money. They'll do all of that dementia. Everybody's afraid that it's gonna happen to them, and there is, quite frankly today, there is no other side. There's no beautiful win at the end of dementia. It is the slow decline and then the loss of life. Um, and so no one would talk. No one knew how to talk to me about it. No one understood when I was talking about it. People in our lives, my mom and I would slowly start to disappear 'cause they didn't know how to be around him. Um, and you can't blame people 'cause it's awkward. We don't talk about it. They don't know. And so it's just layers and layers of grief, um, that happen over time.
